title: Tables
text: For design rules, cell formatting, and border conventions, see Table Standards →

title: Tables
text:
Create your first table
For most VVN projects, use vvn_table(). It handles all styling — maroon header, alternating rows, source line — automatically.

Step 1 — Install gt (once)
install.packages("gt")
gt is the R package that builds the underlying table. vvn_table() wraps it with VVN styling.


Step 2 — Load packages
library(vvnthemes)
library(gt)


Step 3 — Prepare your data
df <- data.frame(
  Region       = c("Urban Core", "Urban Fringe", "Suburban", "Small City", "Rural"),
  `2023 Value` = c(65.3, 61.8, 57.4, 52.1, 46.2),
  Change       = c("+4.1", "+3.9", "+3.5", "+3.2", "+3.0"),
  check.names  = FALSE
)
Sort rows before creating the table — gt displays them in the order they appear in your data frame.


Step 4 — Create the table
df |>
  gt() |>
  vvn_table(
    title       = "Economic Index by Region",
    subtitle    = "Virginia, 2023 estimates",
    source_note = "Source: VVN Example Data"
  )
The pattern is always: data |> gt() |> vvn_table(...). You must call gt() before vvn_table().

title — headline case; displayed in maroon above the table
subtitle — sentence case; geography and year
source_note — required on every published table



Step 5 — Format numbers (optional)
Chain gt formatting functions after vvn_table() to control decimal places, alignment, and separators:
df |>
  gt() |>
  vvn_table(
    title       = "Summary Table",
    source_note = "Source: [Dataset] · VVN Analysis"
  ) |>
  gt::fmt_number(columns = `2023 Value`, decimals = 1) |>
  gt::cols_align(align = "right", columns = -Region)
Common formatting functions:



Function
What it does




gt::fmt_number(columns, decimals = 1)
Round to N decimal places


gt::fmt_percent(columns, decimals = 1)
Format as percentage


gt::fmt_currency(columns)
Format as dollar amount


gt::cols_align(align = "right", columns)
Right-align numbers


gt::tab_spanner(label, columns)
Group columns under a header

title: Tables
text:
Need an interactive table?
If you are building a Shiny dashboard where users need to search, sort, filter, or download data, use DT::datatable() instead of vvn_table().
install.packages("DT")   # once
library(DT)

DT::datatable(
  your_data,
  rownames   = FALSE,
  class      = "stripe hover compact",
  extensions = "Buttons",
  options    = list(
    pageLength = 15,
    dom        = "Bfrtip",
    buttons    = c("csv", "excel")
  )
)
In a Shiny app:
# Server
output$dt_table <- DT::renderDataTable({
  filtered() |>
    DT::datatable(rownames = FALSE, class = "stripe hover compact")
})

# UI
DT::dataTableOutput("dt_table")

Design rules



Rule
Requirement




Title
Headline case, concise


Column headings
Sentence case


Numbers
Right-aligned; consistent decimal places


Missing values
Em dash — — never leave cells blank


Source
Required on every table

title: Tables
text:
Common mistakes
“could not find function ‘gt’” — You forgot library(gt). Run install.packages("gt") first, then library(gt).
“could not find function ‘vvn_table’” — You forgot library(vvnthemes).
vvn_table(df) gives an error. — The correct pattern is df |> gt() |> vvn_table(...). You must call gt() first.
Numbers have too many decimal places. — Add gt::fmt_number(columns = my_col, decimals = 1) after vvn_table().
Table fails VVN review. — You forgot source_note. It is required on every published table.
See Reference Manual → for full parameter documentation on vvn_table() and vvn_table_pvalues().

title: Maps
text: For design rules, legend conventions, and normalization requirements, see Map Standards →

title: Maps
text:
Create your first map
The standard VVN map is a county-level choropleth — Virginia counties filled by color according to a data value. Follow these steps.

Step 1 — Install map packages (once)
install.packages(c("sf", "tigris"))

sf — handles geographic data (shapes, projections)
tigris — downloads official U.S. county boundaries



Step 2 — Load packages
library(vvnthemes)
library(ggplot2)
library(sf)
library(tigris)


Step 3 — Get Virginia county boundaries
va <- counties("VA", cb = TRUE, resolution = "20m") |>
  st_transform(crs = 5070)
crs = 5070 is Albers Equal-Area — the correct projection for static Virginia maps. It makes the state look proportionally correct.


Step 4 — Join your data
# Replace this with your actual data:
# va <- left_join(va, my_data, by = c("GEOID" = "fips"))

# Placeholder so this example runs immediately:
set.seed(42)
va$rate <- runif(nrow(va), 30, 80)






Important



Always map a rate or percentage, never a raw count. Raw counts make densely populated counties look more extreme simply because they have more people, not because the situation is different.




Step 5 — Create and save the map
p <- ggplot(va, aes(fill = rate)) +
  geom_sf(color = "#FFFFFF", linewidth = 0.3) +
  scale_fill_vvn_c("maroon_seq") +
  theme_vvn_map() +
  labs(
    title    = "Broadband Access by County, 2023",
    subtitle = "Percentage of households with broadband access",
    fill     = "Households (%)"
  ) +
  vvn_source("FCC Broadband Data · VVN Analysis")

vvn_save(p, "figures/map.png")

scale_fill_vvn_c("maroon_seq") — light to dark maroon; the VVN standard for county data
theme_vvn_map() — removes axes, places legend at bottom right
fill = "Households (%)" — legend label must include units
vvn_source() — required on every published map

title: Maps
text:
For dashboards: interactive map
If you are building a Shiny dashboard, use vvn_map_style() with Leaflet instead of the static ggplot2 approach. Interactive maps let users zoom, pan, and hover over counties.

Step 1 — Install leaflet (once)
install.packages("leaflet")


Step 2 — Load and prepare data (CRS must be 4326)
library(leaflet)
library(sf)
library(tigris)

va <- counties("VA", cb = TRUE, resolution = "20m") |>
  st_transform(crs = 4326)   # WGS84 — required for Leaflet (NOT 5070)


Step 3 — Create the map
set.seed(42)
va$rate <- runif(nrow(va), 30, 80)

leaflet(va) |>
  vvn_map_style(va, "rate",
                label_col = "NAME",
                title     = "Broadband coverage (%)",
                palette   = "maroon_seq")
label_col = "NAME" — shows the county name in the hover tooltip.


In a Shiny app
# Server
output$county_map <- leaflet::renderLeaflet({
  leaflet(va) |>
    vvn_map_style(va, value_col = input$variable_sel, title = "Selected Variable")
})

# UI
leaflet::leafletOutput("county_map", height = "580px")

title: Maps
text:
Other map types

Point map

Use when Showing specific locations such as facilities, schools, or sample sites.

locations_sf <- sf::st_as_sf(
  your_points_df,
  coords = c("longitude", "latitude"),
  crs    = 4326
)

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = va, fill = "#F7F7F7", color = "#E0E0E0", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_sf(data = locations_sf, color = "#861F41", size = 2.5, alpha = 0.8) +
  theme_vvn_map() +
  labs(title = "Location Map Title", subtitle = "Virginia, 2023") +
  vvn_source("Source: [Dataset] · VVN Analysis")


Bubble map

Use when Showing proportional quantities at specific locations — circle size represents magnitude.

ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = va, fill = "#F7F7F7", color = "#E0E0E0", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_sf(data = centroids_sf, aes(size = your_variable),
          color = "#861F41", alpha = 0.65, show.legend = TRUE) +
  scale_size_area(max_size = 18, name = "Variable label") +
  theme_vvn_map() +
  labs(title = "Bubble Map Title") +
  vvn_source("Source: [Dataset] · VVN Analysis")

title: Maps
text:
Color scale reference







Data type
Scale




Sequential (low → high) — primary metric
scale_fill_vvn_c("maroon_seq")


Sequential — secondary metric
scale_fill_vvn_c("orange_seq")


Diverging (above / below average)
scale_fill_vvn_c("diverging")

title: Maps
text:
Common mistakes
Map is blank or all one color. — You forgot left_join() to attach your data to the county shapes. All counties show the same color when no data is joined.
Leaflet map doesn’t render. — You used crs = 5070 for a Leaflet map. Leaflet requires crs = 4326 (WGS84). Use 5070 only for static ggplot2 maps.
Map fails VVN review. — You mapped a raw count (population, number of businesses) instead of a rate. Always normalize: use rates, percentages, or per-capita values.
Legend label has no units. — Set fill = "Households (%)" (or equivalent) in labs(). The legend must always include units.
vvn_source() is missing. — Required on every published map. Add vvn_source("Source: [Dataset] · VVN Analysis").
See Reference Manual → for full parameter documentation on vvn_map_style().

title: Interactive Data Visualizations
text: The create_vvn_dashboard() function scaffolds a complete R Shiny dashboard in one command. Charts are built once in scripts/analysis.R and saved to figures/ as PNGs. The Shiny app displays those charts alongside reactive KPI cards, a data table, and an optional county map.

title: Interactive Data Visualizations
text:
Required libraries
Install before running create_vvn_dashboard():
# app.R — required
install.packages(c("shiny", "bslib", "bsicons", "dplyr", "readr", "scales"))

# scripts/analysis.R — required
install.packages(c("ggplot2", "dplyr", "tidyr"))

# Optional — uncomment in app.R or analysis.R when needed
install.packages(c("leaflet", "sf", "DT", "gt", "mapshot2"))
vvnthemes is required in both app.R and scripts/analysis.R and is loaded automatically by the scaffold.








Library
Used in
What it does




shiny
app.R
Builds the interactive web application — reactive UI and server logic


bslib
app.R
Bootstrap 5 layout: page_navbar(), card(), sidebar(), layout_columns()


bsicons
app.R
Bootstrap icon set used in tab labels and button icons


dplyr
both
Data manipulation: filter(), select(), mutate(), summarise()


readr
app.R
Fast CSV loading with read_csv()


scales
app.R
Number formatting: dollar(), comma(), percent() for KPI labels


ggplot2
analysis.R
Build all static charts saved as PNGs to figures/


tidyr
analysis.R
Reshape data between wide and long formats


leaflet
app.R
Interactive county choropleth map (optional — Map tab)

app.R
Read and process spatial data (.geojson, .shp) — required with leaflet


DT

Searchable, paginated data table with CSV and Excel download buttons


gt

Styled summary tables — pairs with vvn_table() for VT-branded headers

title: Interactive Data Visualizations
text:
Create the project
library(vvnthemes)
create_vvn_dashboard("my-dashboard", title = "Virginia Housing Dashboard")

title: Interactive Data Visualizations
text:
Project structure
my-dashboard/
├── app.R                    ← Main Shiny app; load data, display charts, reactive UI
├── scripts/
│   └── analysis.R           ← Build all ggplot2 charts here; source this first
├── figures/                 ← Auto-created when analysis.R runs; holds numbered PNGs
│   ├── 01_trend_statewide.png
│   ├── 02_trend_by_region.png
│   └── ...
├── www/
│   └── vvn.css              ← VVN brand styles (do not edit)
└── data/
    ├── raw/                 ← Original source files (do not edit)
    │   ├── county_data.csv
    │   └── demographics.csv
    └── processed/           ← Cleaned, analysis-ready files (load in app.R)
        └── my_data.csv

title: Interactive Data Visualizations
text:
What each file does
scripts/analysis.R is where you write all chart code. Load your data, uncomment the chart types you need from the gallery (line, bar, scatter, lollipop, histogram, map, …), fill in your column names and labels, then source the script. Figures are auto-numbered 01_, 02_, … and saved to figures/ in the order the script runs. Every chart must have a title, subtitle, and vvn_source() line.
app.R is the Shiny application. At the top, load your data directly from data/processed/ or data/raw/. Charts are displayed as static PNGs from figures/ — update the filename in each renderUI block to match the file saved by scripts/analysis.R. Interactive components (KPI cards, sidebar filters, data table, map) are reactive and defined in the server.
figures/ is populated automatically when you source scripts/analysis.R. Do not edit files here manually.
www/vvn.css is the compiled VVN brand stylesheet. Do not edit this file.

title: Interactive Data Visualizations
text:
Workflow
Follow these steps in order:
Step 1 — Load your data
Uncomment and fill in the data loading section near the top of app.R:
# In app.R — replace with your actual filename:
app_data <- readr::read_csv("data/processed/my_data.csv",
                             show_col_types = FALSE)
Step 2 — Build figures
Open scripts/analysis.R in RStudio. Uncomment the chart types you need and fill in your column names. Then source the script:
source("scripts/analysis.R")
Figures are auto-numbered and saved to figures/ in the order they run.
Step 3 — Wire charts and components in app.R
Update the filename in each renderUI block to match the PNG saved in figures/:
# In app.R server — replace 01_[name].png with your actual filename:
output$chart_a <- renderUI({
  f <- "figures/01_trend_statewide.png"
  if (file.exists(f)) {
    tags$img(src = f, style = "width:100%; display:block;", alt = "...")
  } else {
    tags$p("Chart not found. Run scripts/analysis.R first, then refresh.")
  }
})
Uncomment and fill in the KPI cards, sidebar filters, and data table in the server.
Step 4 — Run and deploy
shiny::runApp("my-dashboard")
rsconnect::deployApp("my-dashboard")

title: Charts
text: For title/label conventions and chart selection rules, see Chart Standards →

title: Charts
text:
Create your first chart
The horizontal bar chart is the most common VVN output. Follow these steps to build one from scratch.

Step 1 — Load packages
library(vvnthemes)
library(ggplot2)
set_vvn_defaults()
Call set_vvn_defaults() once per session. It sets the VVN theme globally and makes all bars, lines, and points VT Maroon by default — you never have to specify colors manually.


Step 2 — Prepare your data
df <- data.frame(
  region = c("Rural", "Small City", "Suburban", "Urban Fringe", "Urban Core"),
  value  = c(46.2, 52.1, 57.4, 61.8, 65.3)
)
Sort rows from smallest to largest value before plotting so bars display in order.


Step 3 — Build the chart
p <- ggplot(df, aes(x = value, y = reorder(region, value))) +
  geom_col(width = 0.65) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(value, 1)), hjust = -0.2, size = 3.5) +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, .15)))

x = value, y = reorder(region, value) — value on x, categories on y, sorted
geom_text(...) — adds a value label just past the end of each bar
scale_x_continuous(expand = ...) — adds space to the right so labels aren’t cut off



Step 4 — Add title, subtitle, and source
p <- p +
  labs(
    title    = "Economic Index by Region, 2023",
    subtitle = "Virginia regions ranked by index value",
    x = "Index value", y = NULL
  ) +
  vvn_source("VVN Example Data") +
  remove_ticks()

title — headline case: capitalize every major word
subtitle — sentence case: geography and year
vvn_source() — required on every published chart; adds a gray caption at the bottom
remove_ticks() — standard for bar charts; removes the small tick marks on axes



Step 5 — Save
vvn_save(p, "figures/chart.png")              # 8×5 in, 300 dpi
vvn_save(p, "figures/chart.png", width = 12)  # wider for wide layouts
vvn_save(p, "figures/chart", formats = c("png", "pdf"))  # both formats

title: Charts
text:
Which chart should I make?








I want to show…
Chart type
Key function




Ranking across 5+ categories
Horizontal bar
geom_col() (bars go left-right)


Comparison of 3–4 short-named groups
Vertical column
geom_col() (bars go up)


Change over time
Line chart
geom_line() + geom_point()


Distribution of one variable
Histogram
geom_histogram()


Distributions across groups
Boxplot
geom_boxplot()


Relationship between two variables
Scatter plot
geom_point()

title: Charts
text:
More chart types

Line chart

Use when Showing change over time for one or more groups. One line per group.

df_trend <- data.frame(
  year  = rep(2018:2023, 2),
  value = c(44, 46, 47, 49, 51, 53, 62, 63, 65, 64, 67, 70),
  group = rep(c("Rural", "Urban"), each = 6)
)

ggplot(df_trend, aes(x = year, y = value, color = group, group = group)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  scale_color_vvn("vt") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2018:2023) +
  labs(
    title    = "Broadband Access Over Time",
    subtitle = "Percentage of households, Virginia 2018–2023",
    x = NULL, y = "Households (%)", color = NULL
  ) +
  vvn_source("FCC Broadband Data") +
  legend_bottom()






Tip



For direct labels instead of a legend, use ggrepel::geom_text_repel() on the last year’s values and add remove_legend().




Scatter plot

Use when Exploring the association between two continuous variables. Always add grid lines on both axes.

ggplot(df, aes(x = income, y = broadband)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title    = "Income and Broadband Access",
    subtitle = "Virginia counties, 2022",
    x = "Median household income", y = "Households with broadband (%)"
  ) +
  vvn_source("U.S. Census Bureau, ACS 5-Year Estimates, 2022") +
  scatter_grid()
scatter_grid() adds grid lines on both axes — required for scatter plots so readers can trace values.


Histogram

Use when Showing the distribution of a single continuous variable. Add a mean line for context.

m <- mean(df$value, na.rm = TRUE)

ggplot(df, aes(x = value)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 25, fill = "#861F41", color = "#FFFFFF", alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = m, color = "#E5751F", linewidth = 1.2, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = m + 0.5, y = Inf,
           label = paste0("Mean: ", round(m, 1)),
           color = "#E5751F", hjust = 0, vjust = 1.5, size = 3.5) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Values", x = "Value", y = "Count") +
  vvn_source("Source: [Dataset] · VVN Analysis")


Boxplot

Use when Comparing distributions across two or more groups. Shows median, IQR, and outliers.

ggplot(df, aes(x = region, y = value, fill = region)) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.5, outlier.color = "#333333",
               outlier.size = 1.5, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_vvn("vt") +
  labs(title = "Value Distribution by Region", x = NULL, y = "Value") +
  vvn_source("Source: [Dataset] · VVN Analysis") +
  remove_ticks()


Highlight one group

Use when Drawing attention to a focal category — for example, Rural vs. all others.

df_latest <- df |>
  filter(year == max(year)) |>
  arrange(value) |>
  mutate(region = factor(region, levels = region),
         highlight = region == "Rural")

ggplot(df_latest, aes(x = value, y = region, fill = highlight)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.65) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(`TRUE` = "#E5751F", `FALSE` = "#861F41"),
                    guide  = "none") +
  labs(title    = "Regional Comparison, 2023",
       subtitle = "Rural highlighted in orange",
       x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  vvn_source("Source: [Dataset] · VVN Analysis") +
  remove_ticks()

title: Charts
text:
Color palettes
Use scale_fill_vvn("palette") for discrete fills (bars, areas) and scale_color_vvn("palette") for discrete colors (lines, points). Use scale_fill_vvn_c("palette") / scale_color_vvn_c("palette") for continuous data.


Categorical palettes — scale_fill_vvn() / scale_color_vvn()
"vt" — Official VT Brand (6 colors)






Chicago Maroon #861F41







Impact Orange #CA4F00







Pylon Purple #642667







Sustainable Teal #508590







Hokie Stone #75787B







Land Grant Grey #D7D2CB



scale_fill_vvn("vt")    # bars, areas
scale_color_vvn("vt")   # lines, points

Artistic themes — editorial or design-forward charts






"monet" · 5 colors Blues, sage, cream







"sunflower" · 5 colors Cobalt + gold







"academic" · 5 colors Navy + amber







"natural" · 5 colors Sage, slate, linen



scale_fill_vvn("monet")       # Monet Pond
scale_fill_vvn("sunflower")   # Van Gogh Sunflowers
scale_fill_vvn("academic")    # Academic Deep Blue
scale_fill_vvn("natural")     # Natural Muted



Sequential palettes — scale_fill_vvn_c() / scale_color_vvn_c()
For continuous data: maps, heatmaps, density plots.





"maroon_seq" — Light pink → VT Maroon · Primary choropleth







"orange_seq" — Cream → VT Orange · Secondary metric







"navy_seq" — Sky blue → Navy · Tertiary metric







"gray_seq" — Light → Dark gray · Monochrome


scale_fill_vvn_c("maroon_seq")   # most common — choropleth maps
scale_fill_vvn_c("orange_seq")
scale_fill_vvn_c("navy_seq")
scale_fill_vvn_c("gray_seq")



Diverging palettes — scale_fill_vvn_c() / scale_color_vvn_c()
For data with a meaningful midpoint: above/below average, change from baseline, z-scores.





"vt_div" — VT Maroon ↔︎ white ↔︎ Navy · Interpolated to any n







"vt_orange_div" — Impact Orange (#CA4F00) ↔︎ cream ↔︎ Maroon · Interpolated to any n







"diverging" — Navy → white → Maroon · 7-color fixed







"monet_div" — Sky blue ↔︎ cream ↔︎ deep teal







"sunflower_div" — Cobalt ↔︎ parchment ↔︎ yellow







"academic_div" — Deep navy ↔︎ blue-gray ↔︎ amber







"natural_div" — Near-black ↔︎ off-white ↔︎ sage


scale_fill_vvn_c("vt_div")          # classic VT diverging
scale_fill_vvn_c("vt_orange_div")   # orange-anchored diverging
scale_fill_vvn_c("diverging")       # fixed 7-color
scale_fill_vvn_c("monet_div", n = 11)   # interpolated to any n

title: Charts
text:
Common mistakes
Bars aren’t maroon. You forgot set_vvn_defaults(). Add it once at the top of your script.
Value labels get cut off. Add scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, .15))) to create space to the right.
Bars aren’t sorted. Use reorder(region, value) in the aes() mapping, or arrange() + mutate(region = factor(region, levels = region)) before plotting.
Chart fails VVN review. You forgot vvn_source(). It is required on every published figure.
Scatter plot looks empty. You need scatter_grid() or theme_vvn(grid = "both") — scatter plots require grid lines on both axes.
See Reference Manual → for full parameter documentation.

2

3

4

title: Get Started
text:
Step 1 — Install
Install vvnthemes once per machine. You do not need to reinstall it every session.
install.packages("pak")                            # skip if already installed
pak::pak("yujuangao/VVN")
Then add these two lines to the top of every script or Quarto chunk:
library(vvnthemes)
set_vvn_defaults()   # one call transforms every subsequent ggplot2 chart
set_vvn_defaults() does three things: it sets the global ggplot2 theme to theme_vvn(), changes all default geom colors to VT Maroon, and makes sure your charts look like VVN without any extra code.

title: Get Started
text:
Step 2 — Your first chart
Goal: A horizontal bar chart ranking Virginia regions by an index value — white background, VT Maroon bars, maroon title, source line at the bottom.
This is the most common VVN chart type. Copy, run, and you will see the result immediately.
library(vvnthemes)
library(ggplot2)

set_vvn_defaults()

df <- data.frame(
  region = c("Rural", "Small City", "Suburban", "Urban Fringe", "Urban Core"),
  value  = c(46.2, 52.1, 57.4, 61.8, 65.3)
)

ggplot(df, aes(x = value, y = reorder(region, value))) +
  geom_col(width = 0.65) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(value, 1)), hjust = -0.2, size = 3.5) +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, .15))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Economic Index by Region, 2023",
    subtitle = "Virginia regions ranked by index value",
    x = "Index value", y = NULL
  ) +
  vvn_source("VVN Example Data") +
  remove_ticks()
What happened automatically because of set_vvn_defaults():

Bars filled VT Maroon — no fill = argument needed
White plot background instead of ggplot2’s default gray
VT Maroon title and horizontal grid lines only
Professional font sizing and spacing

vvn_source() adds the required source line in gray below the chart. remove_ticks() removes axis ticks — standard for bar charts.

For more chart types (line, scatter, histogram, boxplot), see Chart Library →

title: Get Started
text:
Step 3 — Your first table
Goal: A summary table with a maroon header row, alternating gray body rows, title, and source line — the standard VVN table format.
The gt package is required. Install it once with install.packages("gt").
library(vvnthemes)
library(gt)

df <- data.frame(
  Region       = c("Urban Core", "Urban Fringe", "Suburban", "Small City", "Rural"),
  `2023 Value` = c(65.3, 61.8, 57.4, 52.1, 46.2),
  `Change`     = c("+4.1", "+3.9", "+3.5", "+3.2", "+3.0"),
  check.names  = FALSE
)

df |>
  gt() |>
  vvn_table(
    title       = "Economic Index by Region",
    subtitle    = "Virginia, 2023 estimates",
    source_note = "Source: VVN Example Data"
  )
vvn_table() applies the full VVN table style in one call: maroon header, alternating rows, orange bottom border on the header, and the source note.

For ranking tables, regression tables, and interactive DT tables, see Table Library →

title: Get Started
text:
Step 4 — Your first map
Goal: A county-level choropleth map of Virginia using the maroon sequential palette — light counties have low values, dark maroon counties have high values.
You need county boundary data. The fastest way is the tigris package:
install.packages("tigris")   # once
install.packages("sf")       # once
library(vvnthemes)
library(ggplot2)
library(sf)
library(tigris)
library(dplyr)

# Download Virginia county boundaries
va <- counties("VA", cb = TRUE, resolution = "20m") |>
  st_transform(crs = 5070)

# Join your data (replace with your own)
# va <- left_join(va, your_data, by = c("GEOID" = "fips"))

# For this example, simulate a value
set.seed(42)
va$rate <- runif(nrow(va), 30, 80)

ggplot(va, aes(fill = rate)) +
  geom_sf(color = "#FFFFFF", linewidth = 0.3) +
  scale_fill_vvn_c("maroon_seq") +
  theme_vvn_map() +
  labs(
    title    = "Broadband Access by County, 2023",
    subtitle = "Percentage of households with broadband access",
    fill     = "Households (%)"
  ) +
  vvn_source("FCC Broadband Data · VVN Analysis")
scale_fill_vvn_c("maroon_seq") applies the maroon sequential palette — the VVN standard for continuous county-level data. theme_vvn_map() removes axes and ticks automatically.

For interactive Leaflet maps and point maps, see Map Library →

title: Get Started
text:
Step 5 — Start a full project
Instead of building file by file, scaffold a complete VVN project in one command. The generated project runs immediately with placeholder data.
Dashboard (R Shiny):
library(vvnthemes)

create_vvn_dashboard("my-dashboard",
                      title = "Virginia Housing Dashboard")
# Created: my-dashboard/app.R · R/data_prep.R · www/vvn.css

shiny::runApp("my-dashboard")   # runs immediately
VVN Insights document (Quarto):
create_vvn_story("my-insight",
                  title  = "Broadband Access in Rural Virginia",
                  author = "VVN Research Team")
# Created: my-insight/index.qmd · _quarto.yml · styles.scss
#          scripts/analysis.R · data/raw/ · data/processed/ · figures/
Then follow three steps:
# Step 1 — open scripts/analysis.R, fill in your chart code, then source it
source("my-insight/scripts/analysis.R")
# → figures saved to my-insight/figures/01_name.png, 02_name.png, …

# Step 2 — open my-insight/index.qmd, replace [placeholders] with your text
#           and update filenames in knitr::include_graphics("figures/01_name.png")

# Step 3 — render
quarto::quarto_render("my-insight/index.qmd")

See VVN Insight Library → for the full annotated walkthrough and chart gallery.


See Dashboard Template → and VVN Insights Template → for annotated walkthroughs.

title: vvnthemes
text:
vvnthemes is the design foundation for all VVN projects. It provides ggplot2 themes, brand color palettes, scale functions, Shiny UI components, and project scaffolding — all consistent with the Virginia Tech visual identity.
# Install once from GitHub
pak::pak("yujuangao/VVN")

# Load and apply
library(vvnthemes)
set_vvn_defaults()   # styles all subsequent ggplot2 calls

See Get Started → for a step-by-step workflow guide.

title: vvnthemes
text:
vvnthemes is the design foundation for all VVN projects. It provides ggplot2 themes, brand color palettes, scale functions, Shiny UI components, and project scaffolding — all consistent with the Virginia Tech visual identity.
# Install once from GitHub
pak::pak("yujuangao/VVN")

# Load and apply
library(vvnthemes)
set_vvn_defaults()   # styles all subsequent ggplot2 calls

See Get Started → for a step-by-step workflow guide.

title: vvnthemes
text:
Setup

set_vvn_defaults()
Set theme_vvn() as the global ggplot2 theme and update default geom aesthetics for the session. Call once at the top of every script.

Usage
set_vvn_defaults(style = c("standard", "map"), base_size = 12)


Arguments








Argument
Default
Description




style
"standard"
"standard" applies theme_vvn(); "map" applies theme_vvn_map().


base_size
12
Base font size passed to the theme.





Value
Invisibly NULL. Called for its side effects — sets the ggplot2 global theme and geom color defaults (bars, lines, points → VT Maroon; smooth → VT Orange).


Examples
library(vvnthemes)
set_vvn_defaults()

# All subsequent ggplot2 calls use theme_vvn() and VVN colors automatically
ggplot2::ggplot(mpg, ggplot2::aes(displ, hwy)) +
  ggplot2::geom_point()

undo_vvn_defaults()   # revert when done




undo_vvn_defaults()
Revert to ggplot2 factory defaults, undoing set_vvn_defaults().

Usage
undo_vvn_defaults()


Value
Invisibly NULL.


Examples
set_vvn_defaults()
# ... make VVN charts ...
undo_vvn_defaults()

title: vvnthemes
text:
Themes

theme_vvn()
Standard VVN ggplot2 theme: white background, VT Maroon titles, light gray grid, and clean typography. Use for bars, lines, histograms, and most charts.

Usage
theme_vvn(base_size = 12, base_family = "sans", grid = "y")


Arguments








Argument
Default
Description




base_size
12
Base font size in points.


base_family
"sans"
Font family.


grid
"y"
Grid lines to show: "y" (horizontal only), "x", "both", or "none".





Value
A ggplot2::theme object.


Examples
library(ggplot2)

# Bar chart — horizontal grid (default)
ggplot(mpg, aes(class)) +
  geom_bar() +
  theme_vvn()

# Scatter plot — both axes
ggplot(mpg, aes(displ, hwy)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_vvn(grid = "both")




theme_vvn_map()
Map theme for ggplot2 + sf static choropleth maps. Removes axes and ticks; adds a muted panel background and a horizontal legend at the bottom.

Usage
theme_vvn_map(base_size = 11, base_family = "sans")


Arguments



Argument
Default
Description




base_size
11
Base font size in points.


base_family
"sans"
Font family.





Value
A ggplot2::theme object.


Examples
library(ggplot2); library(sf)
ggplot(va_counties_sf, aes(fill = median_income)) +
  geom_sf() +
  scale_fill_vvn_c("maroon_seq") +
  theme_vvn_map()




theme_vvn_minimal()
Minimal VVN variant: no grid lines, no axes. Use for small multiples, annotation-heavy figures, and inset charts.

Usage
theme_vvn_minimal(base_size = 12, base_family = "sans")


Arguments



Argument
Default
Description




base_size
12
Base font size in points.


base_family
"sans"
Font family.





Value
A ggplot2::theme object.

title: vvnthemes
text:
Color System

vvn_colors()
Return one or more named VVN brand colors as a named character vector of hex codes.

Usage
vvn_colors(...)


Arguments







Argument
Description




...
Color name(s) to return. VT Primary: "maroon", "orange", "hokie_stone", "white", "impact_orange", "maroon_dk", "maroon_lt", "orange_lt". VT Secondary: "purple", "pink", "yellow", "teal", "turquoise", "land_grey", "smoke". Utility: "navy", "charcoal", "gray", "light_gray". If none provided, returns all colors.





Value
A named character vector of hex codes.


Examples
vvn_colors()                                        # all VT primary + secondary colors
vvn_colors("maroon")                               # "#861F41"
vvn_colors("maroon", "orange", "hokie_stone")      # VT primary triad
vvn_colors("impact_orange")                        # "#CA4F00"  digital accent
vvn_colors("teal", "purple", "turquoise")          # VT secondary colors




vvn_palette()
Generate a vector of hex colors from a named VVN palette. Sequential and diverging palettes are interpolated when n exceeds the base palette size. Use pick to extract a single color by position.

Usage
vvn_palette(palette = "vt", n = NULL, reverse = FALSE, alpha = 1, pick = NULL)


Arguments








Argument
Default
Description




palette
"vt"
Palette name — see table below.


n
NULL
Number of colors. NULL returns all base colors. Diverging _div palettes default to 7.


reverse
FALSE
Reverse the palette order?


alpha
1
Opacity, 0–1.


pick
NULL
Integer index to return a single color (e.g. pick = 2 → 2nd color). Overrides n.





Palette names









Category
Palette
Colors
Description




VT Brand
"vt"
6
Official VT colors: maroon, impact orange, purple, teal, hokie stone, land grey



"vt_secondary"
8
All 8 official VT secondary colors


VT Sequential
"maroon_seq"
6
Light pink → VT Maroon



"orange_seq"
6
Cream → VT Orange



"navy_seq"
6
Sky blue → Navy



"hokie_stone_seq"
6
Light gray → Hokie Stone



"gray_seq"
6
Light → Dark gray


VT Diverging
"vt_div"
any n
Maroon ↔︎ white ↔︎ Navy



"vt_orange_div"
any n
Impact Orange (#CA4F00) ↔︎ cream ↔︎ Maroon



"diverging"
7 (fixed)
Navy → white → Maroon


Artistic
"monet"
5
Monet Pond: blues, sage, cream



"sunflower"
5
Van Gogh Sunflowers: cobalt + gold



"academic"
5
Academic Deep Blue: navy + amber



"natural"
5
Natural Muted: sage, slate, linen


Artistic Diverging
"monet_div"
any n
Sky blue ↔︎ cream ↔︎ deep teal



"sunflower_div"
any n
Cobalt ↔︎ parchment ↔︎ yellow



"academic_div"
any n
Deep navy ↔︎ blue-gray ↔︎ amber



"natural_div"
any n
Near-black ↔︎ off-white ↔︎ sage





Value
A character vector of hex color codes (length 1 when pick is set).


Examples
# VT brand
vvn_palette("vt")                        # 6 official VT brand colors
vvn_palette("vt_secondary")             # all 8 VT secondary colors
vvn_palette("vt", pick = 1)             # "#861F41"  Chicago Maroon
vvn_palette("vt", pick = 2)             # "#CA4F00"  Impact Orange

# VT sequential & diverging
vvn_palette("maroon_seq", n = 9)         # 9-step interpolated
vvn_palette("vt_div", n = 11)            # 11-step maroon-white-navy
vvn_palette("vt_orange_div", n = 7)      # 7-step orange-cream-maroon

# Artistic themes
vvn_palette("monet")                     # 5 Monet Pond colors
vvn_palette("monet", pick = 3)           # "#7FA7A6"  deep teal
vvn_palette("sunflower_div", n = 9)      # 9-step diverging
vvn_palette("academic", pick = 5)        # "#F28E2B"  amber




view_vvn_palette()
Display a horizontal color swatch strip for any VVN palette.

Usage
view_vvn_palette(palette = "vt", n = NULL, label = TRUE, title = NULL)


Arguments








Argument
Default
Description




palette
"vt"
Palette name (see vvn_palette()).


n
NULL
Number of colors to display. NULL shows all base colors.


label
TRUE
Show hex codes on each swatch?


title
NULL
Plot title. Defaults to vvn_palette("<palette>").





Value
A ggplot object, printed invisibly. Save with vvn_save().


Examples
view_vvn_palette()                       # "vt" palette
view_vvn_palette("diverging", n = 9)

title: vvnthemes
text:
Discrete Scales

scale_color_vvn() / scale_fill_vvn()
Apply a VVN categorical palette to ggplot2 color or fill aesthetics. scale_colour_vvn() is a British-English alias for scale_color_vvn().

Usage
scale_color_vvn(palette = "vt", reverse = FALSE, alpha = 1, ...)
scale_colour_vvn(palette = "vt", reverse = FALSE, alpha = 1, ...)
scale_fill_vvn(palette = "vt", reverse = FALSE, alpha = 1, ...)


Arguments



Argument
Default
Description




palette
"vt"
Palette name — see table below.


reverse
FALSE
Reverse palette order?


alpha
1
Opacity, 0–1.


...

Passed to ggplot2::discrete_scale().





Palette names (categorical only)








Palette
Colors
Description




"vt"
6
VT Maroon, Impact Orange, Purple, Teal, Hokie Stone, Land Grey — official VT brand


"vt_secondary"
8
All 8 official VT secondary colors


"monet"
5
Monet Pond: blues, sage, cream


"sunflower"
5
Van Gogh Sunflowers: cobalt + gold


"academic"
5
Academic Deep Blue: navy + amber


"natural"
5
Natural Muted: sage, slate, linen





Value
A ggplot2 scale object.


Examples
library(ggplot2)

# VT brand bar chart
ggplot(mpg, aes(class, fill = drv)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge") +
  scale_fill_vvn("vt")

# Multi-series line chart — full VT categorical
ggplot(economics_long, aes(date, value01, color = variable)) +
  geom_line() +
  scale_color_vvn("vt")

# Editorial / design-forward chart
ggplot(df, aes(region, value, fill = group)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_vvn("monet")

title: vvnthemes
text:
Continuous Scales

scale_color_vvn_c() / scale_fill_vvn_c()
Apply a VVN sequential or diverging palette to continuous ggplot2 color or fill aesthetics. scale_colour_vvn_c() is a British-English alias for scale_color_vvn_c().

Usage
scale_color_vvn_c(palette = "maroon_seq", reverse = FALSE, ...)
scale_colour_vvn_c(palette = "maroon_seq", reverse = FALSE, ...)
scale_fill_vvn_c(palette = "maroon_seq", reverse = FALSE, ...)


Arguments








Argument
Default
Description




palette
"maroon_seq"
Palette name — see table below.


reverse
FALSE
Reverse palette?


...

Passed to ggplot2::scale_color_gradientn() or scale_fill_gradientn().





Palette names (continuous)








Category
Palette
Description




VT Sequential
"maroon_seq"
Light pink → VT Maroon (primary continuous metric)



"orange_seq"
Cream → VT Orange



"navy_seq"
Sky blue → Navy



"gray_seq"
Light → dark gray


VT Diverging
"vt_div"
VT Maroon ↔︎ white ↔︎ Navy



"vt_orange_div"
Impact Orange ↔︎ cream ↔︎ Maroon



"diverging"
7-color fixed: navy → white → maroon


Artistic Diverging
"monet_div"
Sky blue ↔︎ cream ↔︎ deep teal



"sunflower_div"
Cobalt ↔︎ parchment ↔︎ sunflower yellow



"academic_div"
Deep navy ↔︎ blue-gray ↔︎ amber



"natural_div"
Near-black ↔︎ off-white ↔︎ sage green



All diverging palettes interpolate to any n via colorRampPalette().


Value
A ggplot2 scale object.


Examples
library(ggplot2)

# Choropleth fill — most common VVN use
ggplot(va_counties_sf, aes(fill = pct_broadband)) +
  geom_sf() +
  scale_fill_vvn_c("maroon_seq") +
  theme_vvn_map()

# VT diverging — z-scores or above/below average
ggplot(df, aes(x, y, fill = z_score)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_vvn_c("vt_div")

# Orange–maroon diverging (e.g., change from baseline)
ggplot(df, aes(county, fill = pct_change)) +
  geom_sf() +
  scale_fill_vvn_c("vt_orange_div") +
  theme_vvn_map()

# Artistic diverging for editorial maps
ggplot(df, aes(fill = value)) +
  geom_sf() +
  scale_fill_vvn_c("monet_div") +
  theme_vvn_map()

title: vvnthemes
text:
Chart Helpers

vvn_title()
Add a formatted title and optional subtitle to a ggplot.

Usage
vvn_title(title, subtitle = NULL)


Arguments








Argument
Default
Description




title

Title string. Use headline case (capitalize all major words).


subtitle
NULL
Optional subtitle. Use sentence case.





Value
A ggplot2::labs() call.


Examples
ggplot(mpg, aes(displ, hwy)) +
  geom_point() +
  vvn_title("Engine Size vs Fuel Economy",
             subtitle = "Motor Trend Car Road Tests, 1974")




vvn_source()
Add a source attribution caption to any ggplot. Required on every published VVN figure.

Usage
vvn_source(source, note = NULL)


Arguments








Argument
Default
Description




source

Source string, e.g. "U.S. Census Bureau, ACS 5-Year Estimates, 2022".


note
NULL
Optional note prepended as "Note: ..." before the source line.





Value
A ggplot2::labs(caption = ...) call.


Examples
ggplot(df, aes(x, y)) +
  geom_point() +
  vvn_source("U.S. Census Bureau, ACS 5-Year Estimates, 2022")

# With a methodological note
ggplot(df, aes(x, y)) +
  geom_col() +
  vvn_source("Virginia Employment Commission",
              note = "Data suppressed for localities with fewer than 100 workers.")




vvn_note()
Add a footnote-style annotation below a ggplot.

Usage
vvn_note(text)


Arguments



Argument
Description




text
Note text. Prefixed with "Note: " in the caption.





Value
A ggplot2::labs(caption = ...) call.


Examples
ggplot(df, aes(x, y)) +
  geom_col() +
  vvn_note("Excludes localities with suppressed data.")




remove_ticks()
Remove axis ticks from a ggplot. Recommended for bar charts and categorical axes.

Usage
remove_ticks(axis = "both")


Arguments








Argument
Default
Description




axis
"both"
Which axis ticks to remove: "both", "x", or "y".





Value
A ggplot2::theme() call.


Examples
ggplot(df, aes(x = value, y = region)) +
  geom_col() +
  remove_ticks()




remove_axis()
Remove an entire axis — text, title, ticks, and line — from a ggplot.

Usage
remove_axis(axis = "x")


Arguments



Argument
Default
Description




axis
"x"
Which axis to remove: "x", "y", or "both".





Value
A ggplot2::theme() call.


Examples
ggplot(df, aes(x, y)) +
  geom_col() +
  remove_axis("x")   # remove x-axis when categories are self-evident




scatter_grid()
Add major x and y grid lines to a ggplot. Use with theme_vvn() for scatter plots where both axes need a reference grid.

Usage
scatter_grid()


Value
A ggplot2::theme() call.


Examples
ggplot(df, aes(income, broadband)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_vvn() +
  scatter_grid()




legend_bottom()
Move the legend to the bottom of a ggplot, oriented horizontally.

Usage
legend_bottom()


Value
A ggplot2::theme() call.


Examples
ggplot(df, aes(date, value, color = group)) +
  geom_line() +
  legend_bottom()




remove_legend()
Remove the legend from a ggplot entirely.

Usage
remove_legend()


Value
A ggplot2::theme() call.


Examples
ggplot(df, aes(region, fill = region)) +
  geom_col() +
  remove_legend()




vvn_save()
Save a ggplot at publication quality with VVN default dimensions (8×5 in, 300 dpi, white background). Supports multi-format export in one call.

Usage
vvn_save(plot = ggplot2::last_plot(), filename, width = 8, height = 5,
         dpi = 300, formats = NULL)


Arguments








Argument
Default
Description




plot
last_plot()
A ggplot object. Defaults to the most recently printed plot.


filename

File path with extension (e.g. "figures/chart.png"), or base path when formats is provided.


width
8
Width in inches.


height
5
Height in inches.


dpi
300
Resolution in dots per inch.


formats
NULL
Character vector of extensions for simultaneous multi-format export, e.g. c("png", "pdf", "svg"). Overrides the filename extension.





Value
Invisibly returns the file path(s).


Examples
p <- ggplot(mpg, aes(class)) + geom_bar() + theme_vvn()

vvn_save(p, "figures/class_bar.png")

# Export to multiple formats at once
vvn_save(p, "figures/class_bar", formats = c("png", "pdf", "svg"))

title: vvnthemes
text:
Tables

vvn_table()
Apply VVN branding to a gt table: VT Maroon column headers, alternating light gray rows, orange accent border, and optional title and source note.
Requires the gt package (install.packages("gt")).

Usage
vvn_table(gt_tbl, title = NULL, subtitle = NULL, source_note = NULL,
          striped = TRUE, compact = FALSE)


Arguments








Argument
Default
Description




gt_tbl

A gt_tbl object from gt::gt().


title
NULL
Table title string. Use headline case.


subtitle
NULL
Optional subtitle. Use sentence case.


source_note
NULL
Source attribution string. Required for all published tables.


striped
TRUE
Apply alternating row shading (#F7F7F7)?


compact
FALSE
Reduce cell padding for dense tables?





Value
A styled gt_tbl object.


Examples
library(gt)

gt(head(mtcars)) |>
  vvn_table(
    title       = "Motor Trend Road Tests",
    subtitle    = "Selected vehicles, 1974",
    source_note = "Source: Henderson & Velleman (1981)"
  )

For extended examples including ranking tables, regression tables, and interactive DT tables, see the Table Library →.





vvn_table_pvalues()
Color-code p-values in a gt regression table: maroon for p < 0.001, orange for p < 0.05.

Usage
vvn_table_pvalues(gt_tbl, col_name = "p.value",
                  thresholds = c("0.001" = "#861F41", "0.05" = "#E5751F"))


Arguments








Argument
Default
Description




gt_tbl

A gt_tbl object, typically piped after vvn_table().


col_name
"p.value"
Column name containing p-values (character).


thresholds
c("0.001" = "#861F41", "0.05" = "#E5751F")
Named vector mapping threshold values to highlight hex colors.





Value
A gt_tbl with highlighted p-value cells.


Examples
library(gt); library(broom)

lm(mpg ~ wt + cyl, data = mtcars) |>
  tidy() |>
  gt() |>
  vvn_table(title = "OLS Regression: Fuel Economy") |>
  vvn_table_pvalues()

title: vvnthemes
text:
Maps

vvn_map_style()
Apply VVN branding to a Leaflet choropleth map: muted CartoDB basemap, VVN palette fill, maroon county hover highlight, popup tooltips, and a styled legend.
Requires the leaflet and htmltools packages.

Usage
vvn_map_style(map, data, value_col, label_col = value_col,
              palette = "maroon_seq", n_bins = 6, title = value_col,
              opacity = 0.75,
              fmt_fn = function(x) format(round(x), big.mark = ","))


Arguments








Argument
Default
Description




map

A leaflet map from leaflet::leaflet().


data

An sf data frame with polygon geometry.


value_col

Name of the numeric column to visualize (character).


label_col
value_col
Column used as the tooltip label (e.g. county name).


palette
"maroon_seq"
VVN palette — any sequential or diverging palette name accepted by vvn_palette(), e.g. "maroon_seq", "orange_seq", "navy_seq", "vt_div", "diverging".


n_bins
6
Number of legend bins.


title
value_col
Legend title string.


opacity
0.75
Fill opacity, 0–1.


fmt_fn
comma format
Function to format tooltip values. Default: format(round(x), big.mark = ",").





Value
A styled leaflet map.


Examples
library(leaflet); library(sf)

leaflet(va_counties) |>
  vvn_map_style(va_counties, "pct_broadband",
                label_col = "name",
                title     = "Broadband coverage (%)",
                palette   = "maroon_seq")

For complete map examples including variable selectors and diverging palettes, see the Map Library →.

Default

vvn_button()
A styled Shiny action button in one of three VVN style variants.

Usage
vvn_button(inputId, label, icon = NULL,

Arguments








Argument
Default

Shiny input ID.

Button label text.

NULL
Optional Shiny icon from shiny::icon().

"primary"
"primary" = maroon fill (Apply Filters); "secondary" = orange fill; "outline" = maroon border (Reset).

           icon = shiny::icon("download"))




vvn_kpi_card()
Render a VVN KPI metric card with a large value, descriptive label, and optional trend indicator. Use three per row — one maroon, one orange, one navy.

Usage
vvn_kpi_card(value, label, trend = NULL, trend_text = NULL,
             color_scheme = c("maroon", "orange", "navy"))


Arguments








Argument
Default
Description




value

Scalar metric value, e.g. "76%" or 42158.


label

Short descriptive label, e.g. "Households with broadband".


trend
NULL
Trend direction: "up", "down", or NULL for no indicator.


trend_text
NULL
Trend annotation, e.g. "+5 pp vs 2020".


color_scheme
"maroon"
Accent color: "maroon", "orange", or "navy".





Value
A Shiny div tag.


Examples

             trend = "up", trend_text = "+5 pp vs 2020")

             trend = "up", trend_text = "+8% vs 2019",
             color_scheme = "orange")
vvn_kpi_card("22.4%",  "Population in poverty",
             trend = "down", trend_text = "-2.1 pp vs 2020",
             color_scheme = "navy")

For dashboard layout with all three cards in a row, see the Interactive Components →.





vvn_accessibility_check()
Compute WCAG 2.1 contrast ratios for a set of hex colors against a background and report pass/fail status.

Usage
vvn_accessibility_check(colors, background = "#FFFFFF", level = c("AA", "AAA"))


Arguments








Argument
Default
Description




colors

Character vector of hex codes to test.


background
"#FFFFFF"
Background hex code to test against.


level
"AA"
WCAG standard: "AA" (≥ 4.5:1 contrast ratio) or "AAA" (≥ 7.0:1).





Value
Invisibly returns a data frame with columns color, contrast_ratio, pass. Prints a formatted pass/fail report via cli.


Examples
# Check the VT palette on white
vvn_accessibility_check(vvn_palette("vt"))

# Check white text on a maroon background
vvn_accessibility_check(c("#FFFFFF", "#F7F7F7"), background = "#861F41")

# Stricter AAA standard
vvn_accessibility_check(vvn_palette("vt"), level = "AAA")

text:
Project Scaffolding

create_vvn_story()
Scaffold a new VVN Insights Quarto project with VVN branding, directory structure, and pre-built narrative sections.

Usage
create_vvn_story(name, path = ".", title = name,
                 author = "Visualizing Virginia's Numbers")


Arguments








Argument
Default
Description




name

Project folder name. Use snake_case (e.g. "childcare_cost").


path
"."
Parent directory. Defaults to the current working directory.


title
name
Story title injected into the YAML front matter and README.


author
"Visualizing Virginia's Numbers"
Author name(s).





Value
Invisibly returns the project path (character). Prints setup instructions via cli.


Details
Creates:
name/
├── index.qmd        ← Edit this file — pre-built Overview, Background,
│                       Findings, Conclusion sections
├── _quarto.yml
├── styles.scss      ← VVN brand SCSS
├── data/raw/
├── data/processed/
├── figures/
└── scripts/


Examples
create_vvn_story("childcare_cost",
                  title  = "Childcare Cost in Rural Virginia",
                  author = "VVN Research Team")

# Render to HTML
quarto::quarto_render("childcare_cost/index.qmd")

For the full annotated Insights template, see VVN Insights Template →.





check_vvn_story()
Validate a VVN Insights index.qmd for required sections and VVN function usage before publishing.

Usage
check_vvn_story(path = ".")


Arguments



Argument
Default
Description




path
"."
Path to the story folder containing index.qmd.





Value
Invisibly TRUE if all checks pass, FALSE otherwise. Prints a pass/fail report.
Checks for presence of: Overview, Background, Findings, Conclusion, theme_vvn, vvn_source.


Examples
create_vvn_story("my_insight")
# ... edit index.qmd ...
check_vvn_story("my_insight")




create_vvn_dashboard()
Scaffold a production-ready R Shiny dashboard with VVN branding: maroon navbar, KPI cards, reactive charts, Leaflet county map, sidebar filters, and a data download tab.

Usage
create_vvn_dashboard(name, path = ".", title = name,
                     author = "Visualizing Virginia's Numbers")


Arguments



Argument
Default
Description




name

Project folder name.


path
"."
Parent directory.


title
name
Dashboard title shown in the maroon navbar.


author
"Visualizing Virginia's Numbers"
Author name(s).





Value
Invisibly returns the project path. Prints setup instructions via cli.


Details
Creates:
name/
├── app.R                  ← Main dashboard (runs immediately with example data)
├── scripts/analysis.R     ← Load and clean your data
├── www/vvn.css            ← VVN brand CSS
├── figures/
└── data/

Examples
create_vvn_dashboard("housing_dashboard",

For the full annotated dashboard template, see Dashboard Template →.

title: Interactive Components
text:
Styled Shiny UI components for VVN dashboards — filters, sliders, buttons, and KPI cards. These are used in the Dashboard Template only, not in VVN Insights documents.
Required packages

title: Interactive Components
text:
Which component should I use?








I need…
Component
Function




Let users pick a category or region
Dropdown filter
vvn_filter()

title: Interactive Components
text:
Quick start — complete sidebar
A standard VVN sidebar with two filters, a slider, and Apply / Reset buttons:
sidebar <- bslib::sidebar(
  title = "Filters", width = 260, bg = "#F7F7F7",

  vvn_filter("region_sel", "Region",
             choices  = c("All", "Rural", "Urban"),
             selected = "All", multiple = TRUE),

  vvn_slider("year_range", "Year Range",
             min = 2015, max = 2023, value = c(2015, 2023)),

  hr(),

  vvn_button("apply_btn", "Apply Filters",
             icon = bslib::bs_icon("funnel-fill"), style = "primary"),
  tags$br(),
  vvn_button("reset_btn", "Reset",
             icon = bslib::bs_icon("arrow-counterclockwise"), style = "outline")
)

title: Interactive Components
text:
vvn_filter() — dropdown filter

Use when Letting users select one or more categories (region, variable, topic). Produces a styled dropdown with a bold label.

vvn_filter(
  inputId  = "region_sel",
  label    = "Select Region",
  choices  = c("All" = "All", "Rural" = "Rural", "Urban" = "Urban"),
  selected = "All",
  multiple = TRUE
)

Parameters









Parameter
Type
Default
Description




inputId
character
required
Shiny input ID (accessed as input$inputId in server)


label
character
required
Display label shown above the dropdown


choices
named vector
required
Options — name = displayed text, value = code


selected
character
NULL
Initially selected value(s)


multiple
logical
FALSE
Allow multiple selections





Access in server
input$region_sel   # character vector of selected values

title: Interactive Components
text:
vvn_slider() — range slider

Use when Letting users set a numeric range or select a single year. Produces a styled single-handle or two-handle slider.

# Range slider (two handles)
vvn_slider("year_range", "Year Range", min = 2015, max = 2023, value = c(2015, 2023))

# Single-value slider
vvn_slider("year_sel", "Year", min = 2015, max = 2023, value = 2023)

Parameters








Parameter
Type
Description




id
character
Shiny input ID


label
character
Display label


min
numeric
Minimum value


max
numeric
Maximum value


value
numeric or length-2 vector
Initial value(s) — use c(min, max) for a range slider





Access in server
# Range: input$year_range is a length-2 vector [low, high]
filtered_data <- reactive({
  df |> filter(year >= input$year_range[1], year <= input$year_range[2])
})

title: Interactive Components
text:
vvn_button() — action button

Use when Triggering a filter action or resetting state. Use “primary” (maroon) for Apply Filters and “outline” for Reset.

# Primary (maroon) — Apply Filters
vvn_button("apply_btn", "Apply Filters",
           icon = bslib::bs_icon("funnel-fill"), style = "primary")

# Outline — Reset
vvn_button("reset_btn", "Reset",
           icon = bslib::bs_icon("arrow-counterclockwise"), style = "outline")

Parameters









Parameter
Type
Default
Description




inputId
character
required
Shiny input ID (accessed as input$inputId in server)


label
character
required
Button text


icon
icon
NULL
Optional icon from bslib::bs_icon()


style
character
"primary"
"primary" (maroon) · "secondary" · "outline"





Apply + Reset pattern
# Filter triggers only on Apply click
filtered <- reactive({
  df |> filter(year >= input$year_range[1])
}) |> bindEvent(input$apply_btn, ignoreNULL = FALSE)

# Reset restores all defaults
observeEvent(input$reset_btn, {
  updateSelectInput(session, "region_sel", selected = "All")
  updateSliderInput(session, "year_range", value = c(2015, 2023))
})

title: Interactive Components
text:
vvn_kpi_card() — KPI metric card

Use when Displaying a headline metric at the top of a dashboard. Use three per row with maroon, orange, and navy color schemes.

vvn_kpi_card(
  value        = "42%",
  label        = "Employment Rate",
  trend        = "up",
  trend_text   = "+3.1% vs 2022",
  color_scheme = "maroon"
)

Parameters









Parameter
Type
Default
Description




value
character or numeric
required
The large metric value displayed


label
character
required
Short description of the metric


trend
character
NULL
"up" · "down" · "flat" · NULL (no indicator)


trend_text
character
NULL
Supporting text (e.g., “+3% vs 2022”)


color_scheme
character
"maroon"
"maroon" · "orange" · "navy"





Three-card layout in a Shiny app
# Server — compute values from filtered data
output$kpi_mean   <- renderUI({
  vvn_kpi_card(round(mean(filtered()$value, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
               "Average Value", color_scheme = "maroon")
})
output$kpi_n      <- renderUI({
  vvn_kpi_card(nrow(filtered()), "Counties", color_scheme = "orange")
})
output$kpi_change <- renderUI({
  d  <- filtered()
  ch <- round(mean(d$value[d$year == max(d$year)], na.rm = TRUE) -
              mean(d$value[d$year == min(d$year)], na.rm = TRUE), 1)
  vvn_kpi_card(
    value        = paste0(if (ch >= 0) "+" else "", ch),
    label        = paste0("Change (", min(d$year), "–", max(d$year), ")"),
    trend        = if (ch >= 0) "up" else "down",
    color_scheme = "navy"
  )
})

# UI — three equal columns
bslib::layout_columns(
  col_widths = c(4, 4, 4),
  uiOutput("kpi_mean"),
  uiOutput("kpi_n"),
  uiOutput("kpi_change")
)

title: Interactive Components
Interactive Components
text:
Complete examples

KPI card row
Three KPI cards at the top of a dashboard — one per color scheme:
library(shiny)
library(vvnthemes)

fluidRow(
  column(4, vvn_kpi_card("76%",     "Households with broadband",
                          trend = "up", trend_text = "+5 pp vs 2020")),
  column(4, vvn_kpi_card("$52,342", "Median household income",
                          trend = "up", trend_text = "+8% vs 2019",
                          color_scheme = "orange")),
  column(4, vvn_kpi_card("22.4%",   "Population in poverty",
                          trend = "down", trend_text = "-2.1 pp vs 2020",
                          color_scheme = "navy"))
)
Use one maroon card, one orange card, one navy card — always in that order.


Minimal Shiny app with sidebar
A complete minimal app — one filter, one slider, Apply + Reset, and a chart output:
library(shiny)
library(bslib)
library(ggplot2)
library(vvnthemes)

set_vvn_defaults()

ui <- page_sidebar(
  title = "VVN Dashboard",
  sidebar = sidebar(
    title = "Filters", width = 260, bg = "#F7F7F7",
    vvn_filter("region_sel", "Region",
               choices  = c("All", "Rural", "Urban"),
               selected = "All"),
    vvn_slider("year_sel", "Year", min = 2018, max = 2023, value = 2023),
    hr(),
    vvn_button("apply_btn", "Apply",  style = "primary"),
    tags$br(),
    vvn_button("reset_btn", "Reset",  style = "outline")
  ),
  plotOutput("chart")
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {
  filtered <- reactive({
    d <- app_data
    if (input$region_sel != "All") d <- d |> filter(region == input$region_sel)
    d |> filter(year == input$year_sel)
  }) |> bindEvent(input$apply_btn, ignoreNULL = FALSE)

  observeEvent(input$reset_btn, {
    updateSelectInput(session, "region_sel", selected = "All")
    updateSliderInput(session, "year_sel",   value    = 2023)
  })

  output$chart <- renderPlot({
    ggplot(filtered(), aes(x = value, y = reorder(region, value))) +
      geom_col(width = 0.65) +
      vvn_source("VVN Analysis") +
      remove_ticks()
  })
}

shinyApp(ui, server)
