title: Reference Manual
text:
Setup

set_vvn_defaults
Set theme_vvn() as the global ggplot2 theme and update default geom aesthetics for the session. Bars, lines, and points become VT Maroon; smooth lines become VT Orange. Call once at the top of every script.

Usage
set_vvn_defaults(style = c("standard", "map"), base_size = 12)


Arguments

style
"standard" applies theme_vvn(); "map" applies theme_vvn_map(). Default: "standard".
base_size
Base font size passed to the theme. Default: 12.



Value
Invisibly NULL. Called for its side effects — sets the ggplot2 global theme and geom color defaults.


Examples
library(vvnthemes)
set_vvn_defaults()

ggplot2::ggplot(mpg, ggplot2::aes(displ, hwy)) +
  ggplot2::geom_point()   # automatically VT Maroon, theme_vvn()

undo_vvn_defaults()       # revert when done




undo_vvn_defaults
Revert to ggplot2 factory defaults, undoing set_vvn_defaults().

Usage
undo_vvn_defaults()


Value
Invisibly NULL.


Examples
set_vvn_defaults()
# ... make VVN charts ...
undo_vvn_defaults()

title: Reference Manual
text:
Themes

theme_vvn
Standard VVN ggplot2 theme: white background, VT Maroon titles, light gray horizontal grid lines, and clean typography. Use for bar charts, line charts, histograms, and most non-map figures.

Usage
theme_vvn(base_size = 12, base_family = "sans", grid = "y")


Arguments

base_size
Base font size in points. Default: 12.
base_family
Font family. Default: "sans".
grid
Grid lines to display: "y" (horizontal only, default), "x" (vertical only), "both", or "none".



Value
A ggplot2::theme object.


Examples
library(ggplot2)

# Bar chart — horizontal grid (default)
ggplot(mpg, aes(class)) +
  geom_bar() +
  theme_vvn()

# Scatter plot — both axes
ggplot(mpg, aes(displ, hwy)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_vvn(grid = "both")

# Larger base size for presentations
ggplot(mpg, aes(class)) +
  geom_bar() +
  theme_vvn(base_size = 16)




theme_vvn_map
Map theme for ggplot2 + sf static choropleth maps. Removes axes and ticks; adds a muted panel background and places the legend at the bottom.

Usage
theme_vvn_map(base_size = 11, base_family = "sans")


Arguments

base_size
Base font size in points. Default: 11.
base_family
Font family. Default: "sans".



Value
A ggplot2::theme object.


Examples
library(ggplot2); library(sf)

ggplot(va_counties_sf, aes(fill = pct_broadband)) +
  geom_sf(color = "#FFFFFF", linewidth = 0.3) +
  scale_fill_vvn_c("maroon_seq") +
  theme_vvn_map() +
  labs(title = "Broadband Access by County", fill = "Households (%)")




theme_vvn_minimal
Minimal VVN variant: no grid lines, no axes, white background. Use for small multiples, annotation-heavy figures, and inset charts.

Usage
theme_vvn_minimal(base_size = 12, base_family = "sans")


Arguments

base_size
Base font size in points. Default: 12.
base_family
Font family. Default: "sans".



Value
A ggplot2::theme object.


Examples
ggplot(df, aes(x, y)) +
  geom_col() +
  theme_vvn_minimal()

title: Reference Manual
text:
Color System

vvn_colors
Return one or more named VVN brand colors as a named character vector of hex codes.

Usage
vvn_colors(...)


Arguments

...
Color name(s) to return. VT Primary: "maroon", "orange", "hokie_stone", "white", "impact_orange", "maroon_dk", "maroon_lt", "orange_lt". VT Secondary: "purple", "pink", "yellow", "teal", "turquoise", "land_grey", "smoke". Utility: "navy", "charcoal", "gray", "light_gray". If none provided, returns all colors.



Value
A named character vector of hex codes.


Examples
vvn_colors()                             # all brand colors
vvn_colors("maroon")                     # "#861F41"
vvn_colors("maroon", "orange", "hokie_stone")   # VT primary triad
vvn_colors("impact_orange")                     # "#CA4F00"  digital accent
vvn_colors("teal", "purple", "turquoise")       # VT secondary colors




vvn_palette
Generate a vector of hex colors from a named VVN palette. Sequential and diverging palettes are interpolated when n exceeds the base palette size.

Usage
vvn_palette(palette = "main", n = NULL, reverse = FALSE, alpha = 1)


Arguments

palette
Palette name. Categorical: "vt" (6 official VT colors), "vt_secondary" (8 VT secondary colors). Sequential: "maroon_seq", "orange_seq", "navy_seq", "hokie_stone_seq", "gray_seq". Diverging: "vt_div", "vt_orange_div", "diverging". Default: "vt".
n
Number of colors to return. NULL returns all base palette colors. Sequential and diverging palettes interpolate when n exceeds the base size.
reverse
Reverse the palette order? Default: FALSE.
alpha
Opacity, 0–1. Default: 1.



Value
A character vector of hex color codes.


Examples
vvn_palette("vt")                    # 6 official VT brand colors
vvn_palette("maroon_seq", n = 9)     # 9-step interpolated maroon ramp
vvn_palette("diverging", n = 11)




view_vvn_palette
Display a horizontal color swatch strip for any VVN palette. Returns a ggplot object that can be saved with vvn_save().

Usage
view_vvn_palette(palette = "main", n = NULL, label = TRUE, title = NULL)


Arguments

palette
Palette name (see vvn_palette()). Default: "vt".
n
Number of colors to display. NULL shows all base palette colors.
label
Show hex codes on each swatch? Default: TRUE.
title
Plot title. Defaults to vvn_palette("<palette>").



Value
A ggplot object, printed invisibly.


Examples
view_vvn_palette()                       # "vt" palette
view_vvn_palette("diverging", n = 9)
vvn_save(view_vvn_palette("vt"), "figures/vt_palette.png")

title: Reference Manual
text:
Scales

scale_color_vvn
Apply a VVN categorical color palette to the color aesthetic of a ggplot2 chart. scale_colour_vvn() is a British-English alias.

Usage
scale_color_vvn(palette = "main", reverse = FALSE, alpha = 1, ...)
scale_colour_vvn(palette = "main", reverse = FALSE, alpha = 1, ...)


Arguments

palette
Palette name: "vt" (6 official VT colors), "vt_secondary" (8 VT secondary colors), or any artistic palette ("monet", "sunflower", "academic", "natural"). Default: "vt".
reverse
Reverse palette order? Default: FALSE.
alpha
Opacity, 0–1. Default: 1.
...
Passed to ggplot2::discrete_scale().



Value
A ggplot2 scale object.


Examples
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(economics_long, aes(date, value01, color = variable)) +
  geom_line() +
  scale_color_vvn("vt")

ggplot(df, aes(date, value, color = group)) +
  geom_line() +
  scale_color_vvn("vt")




scale_fill_vvn
Apply a VVN categorical color palette to the fill aesthetic. Same palettes and arguments as scale_color_vvn().

Usage
scale_fill_vvn(palette = "main", reverse = FALSE, alpha = 1, ...)


Arguments

palette
Palette name: "vt", "vt_secondary", or any artistic palette ("monet", "sunflower", "academic", "natural"). Default: "vt".
reverse
Reverse palette order? Default: FALSE.
alpha
Opacity, 0–1. Default: 1.
...
Passed to ggplot2::discrete_scale().



Value
A ggplot2 scale object.


Examples
library(ggplot2)

# Grouped bar chart — VT brand palette
ggplot(mpg, aes(class, fill = drv)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge") +
  scale_fill_vvn("vt")

# Editorial / design-forward chart
ggplot(df, aes(region, fill = category)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_vvn("monet")




scale_color_vvn_c
Apply a VVN sequential or diverging palette to a continuous color aesthetic. scale_colour_vvn_c() is a British-English alias.

Usage
scale_color_vvn_c(palette = "maroon_seq", reverse = FALSE, ...)
scale_colour_vvn_c(palette = "maroon_seq", reverse = FALSE, ...)


Arguments

palette
Palette name: "maroon_seq" (primary), "orange_seq", "navy_seq", "hokie_stone_seq", "diverging" (for above/below-average data), or "gray_seq". Default: "maroon_seq".
reverse
Reverse palette direction? Default: FALSE.
...
Passed to ggplot2::scale_color_gradientn().



Value
A ggplot2 scale object.


Examples
ggplot(df, aes(x, y, color = value)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_vvn_c("maroon_seq")

ggplot(df, aes(x, y, color = z_score)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_color_vvn_c("diverging")




scale_fill_vvn_c
Apply a VVN sequential or diverging palette to a continuous fill aesthetic. The primary function for county choropleth maps.

Usage
scale_fill_vvn_c(palette = "maroon_seq", reverse = FALSE, ...)


Arguments

palette
Palette name: "maroon_seq" (default), "orange_seq", "navy_seq", "hokie_stone_seq", "diverging", or "gray_seq".
reverse
Reverse palette direction? Default: FALSE.
...
Passed to ggplot2::scale_fill_gradientn().



Value
A ggplot2 scale object.


Examples
library(ggplot2); library(sf)

# Standard county choropleth
ggplot(va_counties_sf, aes(fill = pct_broadband)) +
  geom_sf(color = "#FFFFFF", linewidth = 0.3) +
  scale_fill_vvn_c("maroon_seq") +
  theme_vvn_map()

# Diverging scale (above/below state average)
ggplot(va_counties_sf, aes(fill = dev_from_mean)) +
  geom_sf() +
  scale_fill_vvn_c("diverging")

title: Reference Manual
text:
Chart Helpers

vvn_title
Add a formatted title and optional subtitle to a ggplot.

Usage
vvn_title(title, subtitle = NULL)


Arguments

title
Title string. Use headline case (capitalize all major words).
subtitle
Optional subtitle. Use sentence case. Default: NULL.



Value
A ggplot2::labs() call.


Examples
ggplot(mpg, aes(displ, hwy)) +
  geom_point() +
  vvn_title("Engine Size vs Fuel Economy",
             subtitle = "Motor Trend car road tests, 1974")




vvn_source
Add a source attribution caption below a ggplot. Required on every published VVN figure.

Usage
vvn_source(source, note = NULL)


Arguments

source
Source string, e.g. "U.S. Census Bureau, ACS 5-Year Estimates, 2022".
note
Optional methodological note. Prepended as Note: … before the source text. Default: NULL.



Value
A ggplot2::labs(caption = ...) call.


Examples
ggplot(df, aes(x, y)) +
  geom_col() +
  vvn_source("U.S. Census Bureau, ACS 5-Year Estimates, 2022")

# With a methodological note
ggplot(df, aes(x, y)) +
  geom_col() +
  vvn_source("Virginia Employment Commission",
              note = "Data suppressed for localities with fewer than 100 workers.")




vvn_note
Add a footnote-style annotation below a ggplot (without a source line).

Usage
vvn_note(text)


Arguments

text
Note text. Prefixed with Note:  in the caption.



Value
A ggplot2::labs(caption = ...) call.


Examples
ggplot(df, aes(x, y)) +
  geom_col() +
  vvn_note("Excludes localities with suppressed data.")




remove_ticks
Remove axis tick marks from a ggplot. Recommended for bar charts and categorical axes.

Usage
remove_ticks(axis = "both")


Arguments

axis
Which axis ticks to remove: "both" (default), "x", or "y".



Value
A ggplot2::theme() call.


Examples
ggplot(df, aes(x = value, y = region)) +
  geom_col() +
  remove_ticks()




remove_axis
Remove an entire axis — text, title, ticks, and line — from a ggplot.

Usage
remove_axis(axis = "x")


Arguments

axis
Which axis to remove entirely: "x" (default), "y", or "both".



Value
A ggplot2::theme() call.


Examples
ggplot(df, aes(x, y)) +
  geom_col() +
  remove_axis("x")   # when category labels are self-evident




scatter_grid
Add major grid lines on both x and y axes. Use with theme_vvn() for scatter plots where both axes need a visual reference.

Usage
scatter_grid()


Value
A ggplot2::theme() call.


Examples
ggplot(df, aes(income, broadband)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_vvn() +
  scatter_grid()

# Equivalent to:
ggplot(df, aes(income, broadband)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_vvn(grid = "both")




legend_bottom
Move the ggplot legend to the bottom of the chart, oriented horizontally.

Usage
legend_bottom()


Value
A ggplot2::theme() call.


Examples
ggplot(df, aes(date, value, color = group)) +
  geom_line() +
  scale_color_vvn("vt") +
  legend_bottom()




remove_legend
Remove the legend from a ggplot entirely.

Usage
remove_legend()


Value
A ggplot2::theme() call.


Examples
ggplot(df, aes(region, value, fill = region)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_vvn("vt") +
  remove_legend()   # when colors are labeled directly on chart




vvn_save
Save a ggplot at publication quality with VVN default dimensions (8 × 5 inches, 300 dpi, white background). Supports multi-format export in a single call.

Usage
vvn_save(plot = ggplot2::last_plot(), filename, width = 8, height = 5,
         dpi = 300, formats = NULL)


Arguments

plot
A ggplot object. Defaults to ggplot2::last_plot().
filename
File path with extension (e.g. "figures/chart.png"), or base path without extension when formats is provided.
width
Output width in inches. Default: 8.
height
Output height in inches. Default: 5.
dpi
Resolution in dots per inch. Default: 300.
formats
Character vector of file extensions for simultaneous multi-format export, e.g. c("png", "pdf", "svg"). When provided, overrides the extension in filename.



Value
Invisibly returns the file path(s) written.


Examples
p <- ggplot(mpg, aes(class)) + geom_bar() + theme_vvn()

vvn_save(p, "figures/class_bar.png")

# Custom size
vvn_save(p, "figures/class_bar_wide.png", width = 12, height = 4)

# Export to multiple formats at once
vvn_save(p, "figures/class_bar", formats = c("png", "pdf", "svg"))




vvn_accessibility_check
Compute WCAG 2.1 contrast ratios for a set of hex colors against a background color and report pass/fail status.

Usage
vvn_accessibility_check(colors, background = "#FFFFFF", level = c("AA", "AAA"))


Arguments

colors
Character vector of hex codes to test.
background
Background hex code to test against. Default: "#FFFFFF".
level
WCAG standard: "AA" requires ≥ 4.5:1 contrast ratio (default); "AAA" requires ≥ 7.0:1.



Value
Invisibly returns a data frame with columns color, contrast_ratio, pass. Prints a formatted pass/fail report via cli.


Examples
# Check VT palette on white background
vvn_accessibility_check(vvn_palette("vt"))

# Check white text on maroon background
vvn_accessibility_check(c("#FFFFFF", "#F7F7F7"), background = "#861F41")

# Stricter AAA standard
vvn_accessibility_check(vvn_palette("vt"), level = "AAA")

title: Reference Manual
text:
Tables

vvn_table
Apply VVN branding to a gt table: VT Maroon column headers, alternating light gray rows, orange accent border on the header, and optional title and source note.
Requires the gt package (install.packages("gt")).

Usage
vvn_table(gt_tbl, title = NULL, subtitle = NULL, source_note = NULL,
          striped = TRUE, compact = FALSE)


Arguments

gt_tbl
A gt_tbl object created by gt::gt().
title
Table title string. Use headline case. Default: NULL.
subtitle
Optional subtitle. Use sentence case. Default: NULL.
source_note
Source attribution string. Required for all published tables. Default: NULL.
striped
Apply alternating row shading (#F7F7F7)? Default: TRUE.
compact
Reduce cell padding for dense tables? Default: FALSE.



Value
A styled gt_tbl object.


Examples
library(gt)

gt(head(mtcars)) |>
  vvn_table(
    title       = "Motor Trend Road Tests",
    subtitle    = "Selected vehicles, 1974",
    source_note = "Source: Henderson & Velleman (1981)"
  )




vvn_table_pvalues
Color-code a p-value column in a gt regression table: maroon for p < 0.001, orange for p < 0.05. Pipe after vvn_table().

Usage
vvn_table_pvalues(gt_tbl, col_name = "p.value",
                  thresholds = c("0.001" = "#861F41", "0.05" = "#E5751F"))


Arguments

gt_tbl
A gt_tbl object, typically piped from vvn_table().
col_name
Name of the column containing p-values. Default: "p.value".
thresholds
Named character vector mapping threshold values to highlight hex colors. Default highlights p < 0.001 in maroon and p < 0.05 in orange.



Value
A gt_tbl with color-highlighted p-value cells.


Examples
library(gt); library(broom)

lm(mpg ~ wt + cyl, data = mtcars) |>
  tidy() |>
  gt() |>
  vvn_table(title = "OLS Regression: Fuel Economy") |>
  vvn_table_pvalues()

title: Reference Manual
text:
Maps

vvn_map_style
Apply VVN branding to a Leaflet choropleth map: muted CartoDB basemap, VVN palette fill, maroon county hover highlight, popup tooltips, and a styled legend.
Requires the leaflet and htmltools packages.

Usage
vvn_map_style(map, data, value_col, label_col = value_col,
              palette = "maroon_seq", n_bins = 6, title = value_col,
              opacity = 0.75,
              fmt_fn = function(x) format(round(x), big.mark = ","))


Arguments

map
A leaflet map object from leaflet::leaflet().
data
An sf data frame with polygon geometry in CRS 4326.
value_col
Name of the numeric column to visualize (character). Use a normalized variable — rate, percentage, or per-capita — never a raw count.
label_col
Column used as the tooltip label (e.g. county name). Default: same as value_col.
palette
VVN palette: "maroon_seq" (default), "orange_seq", "navy_seq", or "diverging".
n_bins
Number of legend bins. Default: 6.
title
Legend title string. Default: value_col.
opacity
Fill opacity, 0–1. Default: 0.75.
fmt_fn
Function to format tooltip values. Default: format(round(x), big.mark = ",").



Value
A styled leaflet map.


Examples
library(leaflet); library(sf)

leaflet(va_counties) |>
  vvn_map_style(va_counties, "pct_broadband",
                label_col = "NAME",
                title     = "Broadband coverage (%)",
                palette   = "maroon_seq")

title: Reference Manual
text:
Shiny UI

vvn_filter
A VVN-branded Shiny dropdown filter (selectInput) in a styled container with a maroon label.
Requires the shiny package.

Usage
vvn_filter(inputId, label, choices, selected = NULL,
           multiple = FALSE, width = "100%")


Arguments

inputId
Shiny input ID (character).
label
Display label shown above the dropdown.
choices
Named vector or list of choices passed to shiny::selectInput().
selected
Default selected value(s). Default: NULL (first choice).
multiple
Allow multiple selections? Default: FALSE.
width
CSS width of the filter container. Default: "100%".



Value
A Shiny tagList.


Examples
vvn_filter("year_sel", "Select year",
           choices  = 2018:2023,
           selected = 2023)

vvn_filter("region_sel", "Region",
           choices  = c("All", "Rural", "Urban"),
           selected = "All")




vvn_slider
A VVN-branded Shiny range slider (sliderInput) with maroon track and handle.

Usage
vvn_slider(inputId, label, min, max, value, step = 1, width = "100%")


Arguments

inputId
Shiny input ID.
label
Display label.
min
Minimum slider value.
max
Maximum slider value.
value
Default value. Use a length-2 vector (e.g. c(2018, 2023)) to create a two-handle range slider.
step
Step size. Default: 1.
width
CSS width. Default: "100%".



Value
A Shiny tagList.


Examples
vvn_slider("year_range", "Year range",
           min = 2015, max = 2023, value = c(2018, 2023))




vvn_button
A styled Shiny action button in one of three VVN style variants.

Usage
vvn_button(inputId, label, icon = NULL,
           style = c("primary", "secondary", "outline"), width = NULL)


Arguments

inputId
Shiny input ID.
label
Button label text.
icon
Optional icon from shiny::icon(). Default: NULL.
style
"primary" — maroon fill, white text (use for Apply Filters). "secondary" — orange fill, white text. "outline" — maroon border, white background (use for Reset). Default: "primary".
width
CSS width. Default: NULL (auto).



Value
A Shiny actionButton.


Examples
vvn_button("apply_btn",  "Apply Filters", style = "primary")
vvn_button("reset_btn",  "Reset",         style = "outline")
vvn_button("export_btn", "Download CSV",  style = "secondary",
           icon = shiny::icon("download"))




vvn_kpi_card
Render a VVN KPI metric card with a large value, descriptive label, and optional trend indicator. Use three per dashboard row — one maroon, one orange, one navy.

Usage
vvn_kpi_card(value, label, trend = NULL, trend_text = NULL,
             color_scheme = c("maroon", "orange", "navy"))


Arguments

value
Scalar metric value displayed in large type, e.g. "76%" or "$52,342".
label
Short descriptive label below the value, e.g. "Households with broadband".
trend
Trend direction: "up", "down", or NULL for no indicator. Default: NULL.
trend_text
Trend annotation string, e.g. "+5 pp vs 2020". Default: NULL.
color_scheme
Accent color for the top border and trend icon: "maroon" (default), "orange", or "navy".



Value
A Shiny div tag.


Examples
library(shiny)

fluidRow(
  column(4, vvn_kpi_card("76%",     "Households with broadband",
                          trend = "up", trend_text = "+5 pp vs 2020")),
  column(4, vvn_kpi_card("$52,342", "Median household income",
                          trend = "up", trend_text = "+8% vs 2019",
                          color_scheme = "orange")),
  column(4, vvn_kpi_card("22.4%",   "Population in poverty",
                          trend = "down", trend_text = "-2.1 pp vs 2020",
                          color_scheme = "navy"))
)

title: Reference Manual
text:
Scaffolding

create_vvn_dashboard
Scaffold a production-ready R Shiny dashboard with VVN branding: maroon navbar, KPI cards, reactive charts, Leaflet county map, sidebar filters, and a data download tab. The generated project runs immediately with placeholder data.

Usage
create_vvn_dashboard(name, path = ".", title = name,
                     author = "Visualizing Virginia's Numbers")


Arguments

name
Project folder name (character). Use snake_case or kebab-case, e.g. "housing_dashboard".
path
Parent directory. Default: "." (current working directory).
title
Dashboard title displayed in the maroon navbar. Default: name.
author
Author name(s) injected into the README. Default: "Visualizing Virginia's Numbers".



Value
Invisibly returns the project path (character). Prints setup instructions via cli.


Details
Creates the following structure:
name/
├── app.R            ← main dashboard (runs immediately)
├── R/data_prep.R    ← load and clean your data here
├── www/vvn.css      ← VVN brand CSS
└── data/
    ├── raw/
    └── processed/


Examples
create_vvn_dashboard("housing_dashboard",
                      title = "Virginia Housing Affordability Dashboard")

shiny::runApp("housing_dashboard")       # test locally
rsconnect::deployApp("housing_dashboard")   # publish




create_vvn_story
Scaffold a new VVN Insights Quarto project with VVN branding, directory structure, and pre-built narrative sections (Overview, Background, Findings, Conclusion). The generated document renders immediately with placeholder content.

Usage
create_vvn_story(name, path = ".", title = name,
                 author = "Visualizing Virginia's Numbers")


Arguments

name
Project folder name. Use snake_case, e.g. "childcare_cost".
path
Parent directory. Default: ".".
title
Story title injected into the YAML front matter and hero banner. Default: name.
author
Author name(s). Default: "Visualizing Virginia's Numbers".



Value
Invisibly returns the project path. Prints setup instructions via cli.


Details
Creates the following structure:
name/
├── index.qmd        ← edit this — pre-built narrative sections
├── _quarto.yml
├── styles.scss      ← VVN brand SCSS
├── data/raw/
├── data/processed/
├── figures/
└── scripts/


Examples
create_vvn_story("childcare_cost",
                  title  = "Childcare Cost in Rural Virginia",
                  author = "VVN Research Team")

quarto::quarto_render("childcare_cost/index.qmd")




check_vvn_story
Validate a VVN Insights index.qmd for required sections and VVN function usage before publishing. Checks for Overview, Background, Findings, Conclusion, theme_vvn(), and vvn_source().

Usage
check_vvn_story(path = ".")


Arguments

path
Path to the story folder containing index.qmd. Default: ".".



Value
Invisibly TRUE if all checks pass, FALSE otherwise. Prints a formatted pass/fail report.


Examples
create_vvn_story("my_insight")
# ... edit index.qmd ...
check_vvn_story("my_insight")

title: Get Started
text:
About vvnthemes
vvnthemes is the design system for all VVN projects. It provides Virginia Tech brand colors, ggplot2 themes, map styling, styled tables, Shiny UI components, and project scaffolding — so every chart, map, table, dashboard, and narrative report your team produces looks consistent and publication-ready.


🎨

VT Brand Colors
VT Maroon, Burnt Orange, and Navy applied globally with a single call to set_vvn_defaults(). Discrete and continuous palettes for every chart type.



📊

ggplot2 Themes & Scales
Three themes for charts, maps, and minimal layouts. Categorical and continuous color scales with automatic VT palette mapping.



🗺️

Interactive Maps
One-function Leaflet county choropleth maps with VT branded fill, maroon hover highlight, tooltips, and a styled legend.



📋

Styled Tables
Branded gt tables with maroon headers, alternating rows, source notes, and p-value color coding for regression output.



🖥️

Shiny UI Components
Branded filters, sliders, buttons, and KPI cards for Shiny dashboards — all styled to match VT brand guidelines.



🚀

Project Scaffolding
One-command setup for a complete Shiny dashboard or Quarto narrative report. Runs immediately with placeholder data.

title: Get Started
text:
Installation
install.packages("pak")
pak::pak("yujuangao/VVN")
Then load the package and apply VT brand defaults:
library(vvnthemes)
set_vvn_defaults()   # sets theme_vvn() + VT Maroon geom colors globally
Optional packages (install separately if needed): gt (tables), leaflet + sf (maps), shiny + bslib (dashboards)

title: Get Started
text:
Library Structure
vvnthemes is organized into 9 modules. Each module is independent — use only what your project needs.


Setup
Apply or remove VT brand defaults globally for the session.
set_vvn_defaults() · undo_vvn_defaults()


Themes
ggplot2 themes for charts, static maps, and minimal layouts.
theme_vvn() · theme_vvn_map() · theme_vvn_minimal()


Color System
Access and preview VT brand color palettes by name.
vvn_colors() · vvn_palette() · view_vvn_palette()


Scales
Discrete and continuous ggplot2 color/fill scales using VT palettes.
scale_color_vvn() · scale_fill_vvn() · scale_*_vvn_c()


Chart Helpers
Add titles, sources, and notes; adjust layout; export at 300 dpi; check accessibility.
vvn_title() · vvn_source() · vvn_save() · vvn_accessibility_check() +6 more


Tables
Style gt tables with maroon headers, alternating rows, and p-value highlighting.
vvn_table() · vvn_table_pvalues()


Maps
Build interactive Leaflet county choropleth maps for Shiny dashboards.
vvn_map_style()


Shiny UI
Branded input controls and KPI display cards for dashboards.
vvn_filter() · vvn_slider() · vvn_button() · vvn_kpi_card()


Scaffolding
Generate a complete Shiny dashboard or Quarto narrative report from a VVN template.
create_vvn_dashboard() · create_vvn_story() · check_vvn_story()

title: Get Started
text:
Typical Workflow
For most VVN projects, follow this five-step pattern:


1

Install & load
Install from GitHub once. At the top of every script, call library(vvnthemes) and set_vvn_defaults() — this applies the VT theme and colors to all subsequent charts automatically.



2

Load and prepare data
Import your county-level data. VVN maps require a normalized variable — a rate, percentage, or per-capita figure, never a raw count. Join to va_counties by FIPS code.



3

Build the visualization
Use ggplot2 + theme_vvn() for charts, vvn_map_style() for interactive maps, or gt() |> vvn_table() for tables. Add VVN scales to apply the correct color palette.



4

Add titles and source attribution
Every published VVN figure requires vvn_title() for the headline (title case) and vvn_source() for the data citation. These are non-negotiable requirements before any figure is shared.



5

Save or deploy
Export static figures at 300 dpi with vvn_save(). For dashboards, deploy to shinyapps.io with rsconnect::deployApp(). For narrative reports, render with quarto render.

title: Function Index
text: Quick-reference for every function in vvnthemes. Click any function name to jump to its full documentation in the Reference Manual.

title: Function Index
text:
Setup







Function
Purpose




set_vvn_defaults()
Sets theme_vvn() as the global ggplot2 theme and changes default geom colors to VT Maroon (bars, points, lines) and VT Orange (smooth lines). Call once per script — every chart after this is automatically branded.


undo_vvn_defaults()
Reverts ggplot2 to factory defaults. Use at the end of a script or when switching to non-VVN work.

title: Function Index
text:
Themes







Function
Purpose




theme_vvn()
Standard VVN ggplot2 theme — white background, maroon titles, light gray horizontal gridlines. Use for bar charts, line charts, and histograms.


theme_vvn_map()
Map variant for ggplot2 + sf static choropleth maps. Removes axes, adds muted panel background, moves legend to bottom.


theme_vvn_minimal()
Stripped-down variant — no gridlines, no axes. For small multiples, annotation-heavy figures, or inset charts.

title: Function Index
text:
Color System







Function
Purpose




vvn_colors()
Returns hex codes for named VT brand colors — primary (maroon, orange, hokie_stone, impact_orange), secondary (purple, teal, turquoise, etc.), and utility colors.


vvn_palette()
Returns a vector of hex codes from a named palette ("vt", "vt_secondary", "maroon_seq", "diverging", etc.). Sequential palettes interpolate to any number of steps.


view_vvn_palette()
Renders a horizontal color swatch strip for any palette — use to preview available colors before building a chart.

title: Function Index
text:
Scales







Function
Purpose




scale_color_vvn()
Applies a VVN categorical palette to the color aesthetic. Use for grouped line charts and scatter plots.


scale_fill_vvn()
Applies a VVN categorical palette to the fill aesthetic. Use for grouped bar charts and area charts.


scale_color_vvn_c()
Applies a sequential or diverging palette to a continuous color aesthetic. Use for scatter plots where color encodes a numeric variable.


scale_fill_vvn_c()
Applies a sequential or diverging palette to a continuous fill. The primary function for static choropleth maps.

title: Function Index
text:
Chart Helpers







Function
Purpose




vvn_title()
Adds a headline-case title and optional sentence-case subtitle to a ggplot via labs().


vvn_source()
Adds a source attribution caption. Required on every published VVN figure.


vvn_note()
Adds a footnote-style annotation without a source line.


remove_ticks()
Removes axis tick marks. Recommended for bar charts and categorical axes.


remove_axis()
Removes an entire axis (text, title, ticks, and line). Use when category labels are self-evident.


scatter_grid()
Adds major gridlines on both axes. Shorthand for theme_vvn(grid = "both").


legend_bottom()
Moves the legend to the bottom of the chart in horizontal orientation.


remove_legend()
Removes the legend entirely. Use when colors are labeled directly on the chart.


vvn_save()
Exports a ggplot at 300 dpi, 8 × 5 in, white background. Supports multi-format export in one call (e.g. c("png","pdf","svg")).


vvn_accessibility_check()
Computes WCAG 2.1 contrast ratios and reports pass/fail for AA (>= 4.5:1) or AAA (>= 7.0:1) standards.

title: VVN Insight
text: The create_vvn_story() function scaffolds a complete Quarto narrative report in one command. The analysis and the narrative are kept in separate files so each is easier to work on independently.

title: VVN Insight
text:
Create the project
library(vvnthemes)
create_vvn_story(
  "my-insight",
  title  = "Broadband Access in Rural Virginia",

title: VVN Insight
text:
Project structure
my-insight/
├── index.qmd            # Narrative document — fill in text, insert figure names
├── _quarto.yml          # Quarto configuration
├── styles.scss          # VVN brand styles (do not edit)
├── data/
│   ├── raw/             # Place raw data files here
│   └── processed/       # Place cleaned / analysis-ready data here
├── scripts/
│   └── analysis.R       # Build all figures here, then run this script
└── figures/             # Auto-created when analysis.R runs — do not edit manually

title: VVN Insight
text:
What each file does
scripts/analysis.R is where you write all chart code. It loads your data, builds each figure using VVN functions, and saves numbered PNGs to figures/ automatically. Run this script first, before opening index.qmd.
index.qmd is the narrative document. It contains all section placeholders ready to fill in. Figures are embedded using a file.exists() guard — the document renders cleanly at any stage, even before figures are built. No chart code lives here.
figures/ is populated automatically when you run scripts/analysis.R. Files are named 01_, 02_, … in the order the script runs them.
data/raw/ holds your original data files unchanged. data/processed/ holds cleaned, analysis-ready files. Load processed files in scripts/analysis.R.
_quarto.yml configures the Quarto project: theme, TOC settings, figure dimensions, and output format. You rarely need to edit this file.
styles.scss is the compiled VVN brand stylesheet. Do not edit this file.

title: VVN Insight
text:
Workflow
Follow these three steps in order:
Step 1 — Build figures
Open scripts/analysis.R in RStudio and fill in your chart code. The script contains a gallery of common VVN chart types — uncomment the ones you need and fill in your column names and labels. Then source the script:
# In RStudio: open scripts/analysis.R, then click Source
# Or from the R console:
source("scripts/analysis.R")
Figures are auto-numbered (01_, 02_, …) and saved to figures/ in the order they run.
Step 2 — Write the narrative
Open index.qmd. Replace every [placeholder] with your content. For each figure chunk, update the filename inside f to match the file saved in figures/:
# In index.qmd — replace [name] with your actual figure filename:
f <- "figures/01_trend_statewide.png"
if (file.exists(f)) knitr::include_graphics(f)
The file.exists() guard means index.qmd renders without errors even if some figures are not yet built — missing figures simply leave a blank space. Once the correct filename is set and the figure exists, it appears automatically.
Step 3 — Render
quarto::quarto_render("index.qmd")
In RStudio, press Cmd+Shift+K (Ctrl+Shift+K on Windows).

title: VVN Insight
text:
Publishing checklist
Before submitting a VVN Insight for publication:

scripts/analysis.R runs without errors and all figures appear in figures/
All [...] placeholders in index.qmd replaced
Figure filenames inside f <- "figures/..." in each chunk match actual files in figures/
Hero banner present with title and VVN subtitle
Key finding stated clearly in Overview
Background explains context and audience
Data & Methods table complete with source
At least 2 charts with titles, subtitles, and source lines
At least 1 map (interactive or static)
Summary table with source note
Discussion covers interpretation and limitations
Conclusion with contact information
All chart titles use headline case
All chart subtitles use sentence case
embed-resources: true in YAML (single-file HTML)
